methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $30M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Mazama, a Series A-stage climate and energy company founded by Sriram Vasantharajan, has raised $30M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on a focused peer set of three same-sector, same-stage private companies—Graphyte ($200M), Fourth Power ($150M), and Steady Energy ($110M)—supplemented by a broader climate tech comp universe of eight private firms and one public comparable.

Two triangulation methods apply here. The stage-progression approach uses the $30M total raised as a proxy base and applies a 2.0x multiplier typical of Series A progression, implying a $60M valuation. The peer-median method references the three directly comparable Series A companies, which show a median valuation of $150M with a range of $110M to $200M. These two approaches yield distinct signals: the capital-based method suggests $60M, while peer benchmarking points to $150M, reflecting the gap between Mazama's fundraising to date and the median valuation of its peer cohort.

Synthesizing across both methods produces a triangulated range of $51M (low) to $172M (high), with a median of $105M. The wide dispersion reflects real uncertainty about whether Mazama tracks closer to its capital raise trajectory or to peer median valuations. Key drivers of final valuation will include differentiated technology, revenue traction, and capital efficiency relative to the peer set. Near-term metrics on customer acquisition and burn rate should narrow this range materially.
